Physical examination reveals tenderness and synovitis in multiple metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. There are also subcutaneous nodules present over the extensor surfaces of her arms. Laboratory tests show elevated levels of C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Radiographs of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.F: Calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate crystal deposition disease

question: A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of stiffness and pain in both hands that is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for more than an hour. She reports difficulty in making a fist and occasionally feels fatigued. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases, including her mother who has lupus. Her current medications include ibuprofen as needed for pain. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Hand X-rays reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following findings is most likely to be associated with her condition?

A 38-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of 3 months of progressively worsening joint pain and stiffness. She describes the pain as primarily affecting her hands and wrists, with stiffness lasting over an hour each morning. She reports fatigue and difficulty performing daily tasks. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical examination, both wrists and several proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 76/min, and blood pressure is 115/72 mm Hg. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of swelling and pain in her hands and knees for the past 4 months. She reports that the symptoms are worse in the morning, making it difficult for her to get out of bed, and improve throughout the day. She has also noticed some fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any chronic medications. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as both knees. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), elevated C-reactive protein (CRP), and positive rheumatoid factor. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are also detected. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with a complaint of persistent fatigue and joint pain that has been present for the past several months. She describes the pain as symmetric, affecting her wrists, knees, and fingers, and notes that it is particularly worse in the morning, improving as the day progresses. She denies any recent infections or new medications. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the affected joints. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 90 fL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 55-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She reports that the symptoms are worse in the morning and improve gradually as the day progresses. She has difficulty opening jars and typing at her computer. She denies any recent trauma or fever, but mentions occasional fatigue. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, well-controlled with lifestyle changes. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.8 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of progressive joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists. She reports that the pain is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. Over the past few months, she has noticed deformities developing in her fingers. She denies any recent trauma. Her medical history is significant for hypertension managed with lisinopril. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. ESR and CRP are elevated. Hand X-rays show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a two-year history of pain and swelling in her hands, wrists, and feet. She describes the stiffness as particularly severe in the morning, lasting several hours before improving as the day progresses. She denies fever, weight loss, or skin rashes. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, treated with lisinopril. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as in her wrists and feet. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. An X-ray of her hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following findings is most consistent with the suspected diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman visits her rheumatologist with worsening pain and swelling in both hands and wrists over the past 18 months. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and has noticed firm lumps under the skin near her elbows. She has a family history of rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. Her medication includes ibuprofen, which provides partial relief. On physical examination, there is synovitis in the wrist and metacarpophalangeal joints, along with subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory results reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive joint pain and deformity that has been worsening over the past 5 years. She describes the pain as persistent and aching, particularly affecting her hands and wrists. She also complains of morning stiffness lasting over an hour. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She is currently taking lisinopril and atorvastatin. On examination, there is marked swelling and deformity of the proximal interphalangeal joints and metacarpophalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory results show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-CCP antibodies. Radiographs of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-year history of aching pain in her hands and wrists, which has progressively worsened. She mentions morning stiffness lasting over an hour. Her medical history reveals hypothyroidism, for which she is taking levothyroxine. On examination, there is notable tenderness and swelling of the bilateral wrist and metacarpophalangeal joints. Several firm, non-tender nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Her ESR and CRP levels are also elevated. X-rays of her hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 45-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive pain and stiffness in both hands and wrists, particularly in the morning. She reports that the stiffness lasts for more than an hour each morning. She also notes occasional swelling in her hands and the presence of firm nodules under the skin near the elbows. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On physical examination, there is symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated on the extensor surfaces of her arms. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) are also elevated. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of joint pain and stiffness. She reports that the pain is most severe in the mornings and affects her wrists, fingers, and knees symmetrically. Her morning stiffness lasts for about 2 hours before improving. She denies any recent trauma or fevers. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism. Family history reveals that her mother has rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). X-rays of the hands reveal periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman visits the clinic with a 2-month history of swelling and pain in her wrists and knuckles. She mentions that the pain is worse in the morning and lasts over an hour before improving somewhat as the day progresses. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. She has no history of trauma. On examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Her temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse is 75/min, and blood pressure is 122/78 mm Hg. Laboratory studies show a positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr. Radiographs of her hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to fatigue and joint pain that has been worsening over the past 6 months. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and pain in her wrists, knees, and small joints of her hands. Her past medical history includes hypertension, and her family history is significant for lupus in her mother. Examination shows symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr, and a normal renal function panel. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 44-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 3-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks due to joint pain. She also notes firm nodules on her elbows. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, treated with levothyroxine. She denies fever, weight loss, or a rash. Her family history includes a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Subcutaneous nodules are observed on both elbows. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory results show:

Hemoglobin: 12.5 g/dL
Leukocytes: 7,000/mm3
Platelets: 240,000/mm3
ESR: 50 mm/hr
C-reactive protein: 15 mg/L

Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies are both positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and swelling, particularly affecting her wrists and fingers bilaterally. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour which improves as the day progresses. She denies any fever, weight loss, or skin rashes. She has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. Her family history is significant for an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-year history of symmetrical pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. The pain is particularly severe in the morning and improves throughout the day. She also reports the presence of firm nodules under the skin near her elbows. Her medical history is significant for hypertension managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent infections or trauma. On physical examination, there is synovitis of the wrist, and metacarpophalangeal joints with visible subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which additional finding is most likely to be observed in this patient?

options.D: Uric acid crystals in synovial fluid

question: A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain. She reports experiencing swelling and stiffness in her hands and knees, particularly noted in the morning hours, which gradually improves as the day progresses. She also mentions feeling unusually tired despite adequate sleep. Her medical history is unremarkable except for occasional migraines. On physical examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal normocytic anemia with a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of worsening joint pain and stiffness, particularly affecting her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. She has been experiencing significant difficulty in performing daily activities due to pain and swelling in her joints. Her medical history includes hypertension and hypothyroidism, managed with lisinopril and levothyroxine, respectively. On physical examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, with visible ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-CCP antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and erosions at the joint margins. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of pain and swelling in both hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting approximately 2 hours, which improves with activity. She also noticed small, firm nodules under the skin of her elbows. Her medical history is otherwise unremarkable, and she takes no medications. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On physical examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints, and you note subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She describes the pain as a dull ache that worsens in the morning and gradually improves throughout the day. She also reports stiffness lasting more than an hour after waking up. Over the past few months, she has noticed firm lumps under the skin on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications regularly. On physical examination, there is synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints bilaterally, and subcutaneous nodules are palpable on the forearms. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated ESR. X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old female presents to the clinic with complaints of pain, swelling, and morning stiffness in her fingers and wrists for the past two months. She notices that the stiffness is worse in the morning and improves after about an hour of activity. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not taking any medications. On physical examination, the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands are swollen and tender to palpation. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Antinuclear antibody testing is positive, and rheumatoid factor is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with swelling and stiffness in her hands and wrists that she has noticed over the past 3 months. She reports that the stiffness is most severe in the morning, lasting for over an hour before she can move freely. She has also experienced fatigue and unintentional weight loss. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Physical examination reveals symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
